title: Souls of Black Folk by W.E.B. Dubios Book Review & Insights

transcript.text: Hello, book review, Souls of Black Folk by W.E.B. Dubois.
I listened to this book yesterday while I was mountain biking, and then after I came
Home, so I finished it in one day, got it off of bookshouldbefree.com, the link is below.
It was published in 1908 by W.E.B. Dubois, who was an African American.
He was the first, I skimmed through his Wikipedia, he was the first black man to receive a doctorate
From Harvard, and he helped co-found the NAACP and fought for the Civil Rights Act, which
Passed like a year after he died.
He lived to be 95 years old.
So this is basically a book talking about the sociology and psychology of black Americans,
Especially in slavery and post-slavery times.
And it was very well written, really good vocabulary, and it's an easy read.
So basically, the list of the titles of all the chapters are below, taken off of my Book
Should Be Free page on my site.
It's basically, yeah, it talks about all of the different things that are kind of go on
Inside the minds of black Americans.
And some of the things that I remember him saying were some of the interesting psychological
Things black people have to deal with, especially post-emancipation.
The emancipation of slavery was a complicated time.
They set up the job bureau after they became free to try to help figure out how much people
Should pay him and how to organize all of that.
And there were a whole lot of stories in the book of white people cheating black people
Because it was easy back then, because black people were not very literate.
And they were very, very ignorant.
And that's something that I think people forget, is how literate slaves were.
I mean, how illiterate and ignorant slaves were.
Slaves weren't allowed to read, and they weren't told anything.
They were just told what to do.
And when you have four generations of people in that kind of situation, they're just not
Going to know much.
And that's why, you know, if you see old movies from back in the slavery days, the black people
Would have totally different vocabulary, almost just super simplified English, you know,
Like, yes, I'm a master.
And I got me my chidden and just, you know, butchered grammar and didn't know how to read.
And so they weren't, they needed help when they were emancipated.
And it was easy for black people to take advantage of.
And there was a story of one black guy who got his freedom and worked to buy land, and
He bought the land off of the person he bought the land from three times over.
He paid for it, and then he paid for it again, and then he paid for it again.
And then the white person never even gave him his deed, and he kept working for 30 cents
An hour and never even, never even got the land because he didn't know how it worked.
When they were paid, they, you know, they didn't even really know how money worked.
It kind of gave me a, reminded me of when I went to Australia.
I rode through Australia, and I went to one of these outback, aboriginal reservation towns.
And there are all these aboriginals in this town, and each of them has like 20 dogs.
And in the town, there's one white family, and I had to go down to the house of the white
Family.
White guy had to open up the store for me, and an aboriginal came.
And he grabbed a bunch of stuff, and then threw a bunch of money on the counter, and
Then the white guy had to count the money and tell him what he had to put back and help
Him figure out, you know, what to buy, because he didn't know how to count the money.
And that's kind of, I think, how a lot of these emancipated slaves were.
They were just kind of helpless, and they needed help.
And Dubois was admitting that they even educated white people looking after them would be better
For them than if they were to just take everything over themselves.
It kind of reminds me of Tanzania, when they got kind of more freedom for the blacks.
Then they came in and robbed the farms off the white people who had been farming for generations
And knew how to do it, and then they just didn't know how to do it.
And so all the farms shut down, because they didn't know how to farm, and then the country
Went down.
Yeah, and then he was talking about some other psychological things.
Being slaves for so long, it's not good for, they have a long way to climb.
It's not good for you to be a slave.
And it kind of takes personal responsibility away.
He was mentioning that black people, after they were emancipated, kind of, they didn't
Have any reason to have moral fortitude, have good morals and know the difference between
Right and wrong.
They just, a lot of them came free, and then they just did what they could get away with
What they were doing, so there was a huge rise in crime from black people just running
Around and, you know, raping people, white people, and stealing from people.
But that was to be expected, Dubois said, because there was no, there wasn't an infrastructure
Of law enforcement in the South down there, because the law enforcement was the slave owners
Whipping them and keeping them in line, but now that they're free, they, a lot of them
Ran wild and became criminals, but a lot of them, you know, obviously weren't criminals
And were good, and they did kind of have a system where any white person in the South
Could stop any black person and ask them whatever they wanted, interrogate them and ask them,
You know, like, are you the real owner of that, and, you know, don't get, and tell them that
They couldn't, there were even laws, they couldn't get sassy with them.
It was, they're actually protected by law, and so it was, and it's very disrespectful to
The blacks, and they didn't like that. My ideas about that are, yeah, that's, I don't
Think that was good, that gives white people the right to continue to walk all over them,
Because there were a lot of poor white people in the South, Dubois was saying, who didn't
Like black people, were very angry with them, because they're ignorant, and they didn't know,
They thought that black people were dumb because they were black, not because they'd been slaves
For so long, but also because there were competition for them for the farming, and, but they were
Supported by the law enforcement. Whenever they, there was, it was hard to get justice for
Black people because, um, in the courts, uh, people wouldn't, would always side with the
White witnesses, and, uh, the white people would, but then the blacks would always side
With the black side, no matter who was at fault, so it was really hard to, to determine, you
Know, what actually happened if there was, uh, some kind of, you know, conflict or crime going
On. It was very, uh, racially, um, divided. And also amongst the, uh, demographics, uh, he
Said that in a, in a town, some example town, um, there was actually a street that you could,
Like a specific street, and on one side of the street, 90% of the people were black, and
On the other side of the street, 90% of the people were white, and I think that was pretty
Typical. Um, he said that a lot of, uh, good white neighborhoods were in the middle
Of black, uh, like good black neighborhoods, um, but there were no good black neighborhoods
Inside, next to the good white neighborhoods. Um, so the, the, the white people were, were
Very segregated from the good black people, so they didn't really know, um, you know, they
Didn't have much, uh, contact with, uh, you know, respectable black people, so it was
Kind of hard to have them, give them a reason to, you know, help black people come up, which
Was different from in the slavery days when, uh, the best of the black people were, um, the,
Uh, the house slaves, and that lived with the white people. So, in some ways, slavery was,
Uh, uh, uh, more, uh, pleasant for some people. Um, and he was mentioning that, uh, some slaves
Actually, what, he interviewed one old man who was like 95 years old, and he said he liked
The slavery days better. If you had a good master who took care of you and, you know, uh,
Made all the decisions for you, and all you had to do was work and not worry about anything,
And you were happy, and then you're, you're free, and all of a sudden you have to, you know,
Fin for yourself legally and against all the whites who don't like you, and, uh, and, or
Whites who were maybe ripping you off. That could be, uh, difficult. So there were some black people
Who, who liked slavery more, um, and so that was kind of one reason why some people, you know,
White people, uh, would try to say that, you know, slavery days were better.
Um, he gave some, a story about how, uh, a lot of stories actually about how black people
Were cheated, um, on, you know, like having to buy more loans and, like, buy, take out
Loans and, uh, then have to pay more than they were told and paid, paid way less than they
Were, uh, told they were going to be paid or less than what they deserved. Uh, one black
Guy, um, the, uh, the sheriff came and, uh, I guess some landowner said he owed money, so the
Sheriff came and took all of his furniture, and that was against the law, and Dubois knew that and
Told him, and the guy said, well, they took my furniture anyway, the sheriff did, and so, you know,
What am I going to do, so, um, this was the first book on bookshouldbeerfree.com that I read
That was, uh, read by a black person, which I liked. The other, the other books, uh, narrative
Of Frederick Douglass and, uh, slave narratives were read by white people, and so, and the person
Who read it knew how to, uh, do the, um, the black accents from back then, the slave accents,
Which was interesting. Another interesting thing he said, which I thought was really interesting,
And that I never really thought about, was that, uh, they had, uh, kept their, uh, slavery,
Their African songs, and the person who read the book sung the African song, and it was, uh,
It was some kind of, um, lullaby, you know, maybe like, uh, 30 seconds, a minute long, or something
Like that, and, uh, and so Dubois, who wrote the book in 1908, um, you know, wrote it in
The book, who, and he read it, he heard it from one of the slaves, so, you know, late 1800s,
They were still, uh, they had hung on to the same song that they, he said that they had,
Uh, for 250 years before that, so, yeah, like, uh, you know, in the late 1600s, or whenever
They're bringing the, uh, the slaves over from Africa, they, uh, they didn't speak English,
They spoke their African language, and, uh, and would sing their songs to their children,
And their children would sing to their children, and by 1800, they didn't even know what the
Songs meant, but they knew the words, and, uh, and when the, the reader of the book wrote,
Read it, it sounded African to me, because I've heard African songs, I mean, African words,
You know, like, it's just interesting to hear that, you know, that even in the late 1800s,
They still, they could still, they still had that connection, you know, I wonder if there's
Any black families alive today who, who sing the song,
Just passed down, not because they read it on Wikipedia, or heard it on YouTube, or something,
But because their father stuck it to them, because his father, and his father, and his
Father, and they just passed down, and, um, yeah, and it kind of makes it, you know, makes
It real and solid, um, remembering that black people were actually stolen from Africa and
Brought here, they're Africans, uh, we're so far in the future, you know, black people are
Just Americans, you know, but, but, uh, they still do speak differently, you know, like,
I could tell, even the guy who wrote the book was, I could barely tell he was black, but
You can still tell somebody, and a lot of blacks, you know, like, uh, rappers, and sports stars,
You hear them talk, and you, you know, they're, they're blacks, and even their grammar is different,
And, um, it's a totally different, uh, language, and, uh, you know, in 2013, and they became,
Uh, free 150 years ago, so, uh, and, you know, between the time where they, now, and when
They became free is less time from when they became free and when they were first brought
Over from, uh, from Africa, I think, so, um, so it's, uh, it's not, it hasn't been that
Long, and, uh, but they've been here, they were slaves for a long time, and, um, but, you
Know, we're not, we have all this technology around us, but we're still, uh, connected to
That, to the past, and to a past that was so different from what is now, and horrific,
Um, he, Dubois gave one story of, uh, of a judge, um, and I don't know what they were
Talking about at the court, but the judge said, um, told the black guy, look, uh, this is,
Uh, this is a white man's country, and white men are meant to rule this country, and you're
Meant to be the subordinate, and that's just how it is, and if you think that you're not,
Then there's, there's gonna be problems, boy, so what do you say, boy, you, is you, do you
Agree with me or not, and the guy said, look, the black man said, look, I'll just do what
I do to survive, and the judge said, all right, well, this is what you're gonna do, so, you
Know, now, if this is the late 1800s, just after slavery, I wouldn't have said that as
A judge, but I can understand maybe how, how a white judge would say that, because, you
Know, you're surrounded by a lot of angry black people, that's what Dubois was saying, angry
Black people who kind of see their whole, uh, situation in slavery as, as being a, um, an
Evil portrayed, perpetrated on them, and, and some of, you know, it's understandable
To see that a lot of them would blame the whites and just want to, you know, kind of
Get recompensed from the whites and rob them, and so, you know, you can't have a bunch of
Black people against whites who are also, don't understand the ways of the whites, which
Is what, and all the technology and the culture, which took a long time to develop, to create
All the technology and the systems that they had, you know, we didn't, they didn't want
America to turn into another Africa, they didn't want, you know, because Africa is a lot more
Primitive, so, you know, there's white culture in Europe and African culture in Africa, and
The Europeans brought their European culture to America, and they wanted to keep that culture,
That European culture in America, so, they wanted to just make sure that the whites, the blacks,
Did what the whites, did it the white way, and the only way for the blacks to do it the
White way is if the blacks are subordinate to the whites, because if the blacks aren't,
Then they'll do it the black way, which is the African way, and, yeah, so, but, but anybody
Who thinks that now, that's just not, that's, that's, it's ridiculous, because they were brought
Here from Africa in, like, 1600s, they've been here hundreds and hundreds of years, they've
Been free for 150 years, white black people have as much of a right to be the leaders of
The country as the white people, or all Americans, and the black people were cheated for a long
Time, and, uh, it was, it was their, their labor that, um, created the economy, that grew
America, and so, uh, they deserve as much, and they are as smart as we are, um, it just
Takes a while for, uh, people to, um, to become educated, especially, and, you know, and it's
Not something that you can abolish in one generation, if you're, if, no matter what race you are,
If you're, if you're raised by an illiterate father who didn't know anything, you know,
And your, your whole world is shaped by that, and your whole surroundings, uh, your children
Are going to, uh, reap the consequences of that, you're not going to be able to, to, uh,
To, to change it in one generation, unless you, you know, unless you don't have kids until
You're like 45 or 50, and you've already established yourself, and you've been busting
Your ass your entire life studying, but that's not very likely, so, I mean, it's, it's just
Something that changes, that takes generations, I think, and, uh, but, you know, after, like,
Four generations, after, like, 150 years, it's, you know, we should, we should treat blacks
Like Americans, and whites like Americans, and treat everybody the same, um, but, yeah, that's
Just an over-civilification of the book, just the most, uh, important, uh, points that
I remembered from it, um, but, uh, he did, um, explain a lot of, kind of, what goes on
In the minds of blacks, um, how, uh, kind of, they had to, you know, in the world, they
Had to figure out why they were in the situation that they were in, and, um, and, uh, you know,
How, how, how there could be a god if, if you make someone, man, greater, you know, better
Than the other, how could that be, and, and, uh, this is one of the, uh, important books
Of, uh, black, black history, so, uh, if you read one, this would be a, it's a good, easy
Read, I got through it, listening to it one day, uh, I don't know how many pages it is, but
I'll put the information below, and the links, and everything, uh, it was interesting, um,
All right, peace out.
